topic: Area of Study 3: Rhythms of the World
subject: music

items.q: What is characteristic of Baroque music?
items.correct: Continuous bass line, ornamentation, terraced dynamics, and counterpoint.
items.why: Students project later styles backwards. Baroque (1600-1750) used basso continuo, harpsichord/organ, and terraced (sudden) dynamics. Piano hadn't yet replaced harpsichord.

items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: Which Paul Simon album famously incorporated South African musical styles?
items.why: Correct answer: Graceland. “Hearts and Bones” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which texture features simultaneous variations of a single melody?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Heterophony. “Homophony” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year was Paul Simon's 'Graceland' released?
items.why: Exact answer: 1986. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
